title: Cat Licked Glow Stick Fluid: Triage Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your cat licked a glow stick, don't panic. Learn why they are drooling and how to help your pet at home. Urgency Level: Low.
content_html:
TL;DR: While the liquid inside glow sticks is extremely bitter and causes dramatic drooling, it is generally non-toxic; however, seek veterinary care if your cat swallows plastic pieces or exhibits persistent vomiting.

What chemicals are inside a glow stick and are they toxic to my cat?

Glow sticks typically contain a chemical called dibutyl phthalate. While this substance is not technically a poison, it is extremely bitter and irritating to the mucous membranes. When a cat punctures a glow stick, the fluid creates an immediate, unpleasant sensation in the mouth.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my cat punctures a glow stick?

  • The urgency level for this situation is Low; while the reaction looks dramatic, it is rarely life-threatening.
  • Foaming at the mouth and frantic pawing is simply the cat's natural way of trying to flush the bitter taste away.
  • Seek immediate care if your cat experiences persistent vomiting or refuses to eat for more than 12 hours.
  • Contact a vet immediately if you suspect they swallowed large pieces of the plastic casing.

How does a photo of the glow stick help with veterinary triage?

  • A photo allows the professional to confirm specific ingredients and ensure no additional additives are present.
  • A photo or video of your cat’s behavior helps the vet determine if the reaction is standard oral irritation or something more concerning that requires an in-person exam.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of glow stick fluid by cats, clinical signs commonly include nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, typically resulting in only mild local irritation. Mild ocular irritation with lacrimation and blepharospasm is also possible. Treatment involves dilution with milk or water to reduce the risk of spontaneous vomiting. Vomiting is usually self-limiting and responds to short periods of food and water restriction. In severe cases, antiemetics (e.g., metoclopramide, 0.2-0.4 mg/kg, PO, SC, or IM, qid) may be required. In rare instances, parenteral fluid therapy may be necessary. Ocular exposures should be treated with 10-20 minutes of ocular irrigation with physiologic saline, followed by fluorescein staining to detect corneal injury.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3004)

faqs.question: Why is my cat foaming at the mouth?
faqs.answer: This is an automatic response to the extremely bitter taste of the dibutyl phthalate in the glow stick fluid. It is the body's attempt to dilute and remove the chemical.

faqs.question: How can I stop the drooling at home?
faqs.answer: You can help by offering your cat something tasty to drink or eat, such as tuna juice, low-sodium chicken broth, or wet food. This helps mask the bitter taste and clear the mouth.

faqs.question: Should I wash my cat's fur?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is important to wipe your cat's fur and paws with a damp cloth to remove any glowing residue. This prevents them from re-ingesting the fluid while grooming later.
